metadata.description: Manuscript-grounded claims for Paper 1 positioning / decision-rule audit traceability

claims.statement: Clinical AI governance instruments specify what to evaluate but commonly underspecify institutional logic that converts heterogeneous evidence into a binary deployment (go/no-go) decision.
Compensatory (weighted or composite) aggregation creates a structural risk: a serious gap in one governance domain may be offset by strong performance in others.

A targeted document audit found that, among the reviewed instruments, none specifies a complete, auditable deployment decision rule combining activation thresholds, non-compensatory logic, and constrained override governance (evidence table: Multimedia Appendix 5).
This Viewpoint proposes a non-compensatory (conjunctive) governance gate architecture across five domains, each with a minimum evidence floor that blocks deployment when failed.
The architecture specifies risk-tiered evidence floors, a constrained override pathway for Gates 2–4, and a shared governance service model for capacity-limited institutions.

The companion simulation study [25] is cited as showing that, under the tested heterogeneous evidence model, the gate structure substantially reduces unsafe deployment rates relative to compensatory composite scoring at a moderate threshold.
A retrospective historical replay evaluation is cited as preliminary convergent evidence, with structural caveats deferred to the companion study.

The decision-rule gap audit coded four elements: (1) explicit go/no-go deployment rule, (2) aggregation logic, (3) override mechanism and constraints, (4) assignment of final deployment authority (full coding frame: Multimedia Appendix 4).
manuscript_anchors: Situating the Contribution: Decision-Rule Gap Audit

claims.statement: Coding was limited to explicit operational statements in instrument text; items were coded “present” only when decision logic was described in operational terms (thresholds, pass/fail, defined aggregation), not as high-level lifecycle principles alone.
Table 1 reports audit findings on the four elements for FUTURE-AI, TEHAI, NICE ESF, FDA AI-DSF, EU AI Act, ISO/IEC 42001, and the proposed Viewpoint architecture (author interpretive assessment).

Among the reviewed instruments, the dominant pattern is rich specification of what to evaluate and lifecycle controls, with comparatively limited operational specification of how evidence is combined into a binding deployment decision.
manuscript_anchors: Audit findings (synthesis paragraph before Decision-Theoretic Rationale)

claims.statement: No reviewed framework combined explicit activation thresholds, non-compensatory logic, and constrained override governance within a single auditable deployment decision structure (Table 1; Multimedia Appendix 5).

A conjunctive rule is argued to be appropriate when failure in any single dimension can produce irreversible harm and accountability must remain clearly attributable; the paper acknowledges a false-positive vs false-negative asymmetry.
Five gates are defined with pathway outcomes; Gates 1 (clinical safety) and 5 (monitoring) are non-overridable; Gates 2–4 are overridable only under stated constrained criteria.
Table 2 specifies risk-tiered minimum evidence thresholds (standard vs higher-risk floors) for each domain.

The constrained override pathway requires four operative criteria to be satisfied simultaneously, plus three structural safeguards against override accumulation (provisional defaults subject to pilot calibration).

The Epic Sepsis illustration argues likely deployment-blocking failures would surface at Gates 1, 3, and 5 under the narrative mapping; the manuscript explicitly states this does not demonstrate categorically better patient outcomes.
FUTURE-AI’s recommendations are mapped to the five gate domains as compatible complementary evidence domains rather than a competing standard.
manuscript_anchors: Relationship to FUTURE-AI and Recent Governance Literature

claims.statement: Preliminary PhysioNet benchmark discussion claims the gate framework discriminates governance dimensions across models and tightens with profile stringency; expanded case counts and limitations are referenced to supplementary materials.
manuscript_anchors: Preliminary Validation Using PhysioNet Clinical Benchmark Data

claims.statement: Scope boundaries: passing all gates is necessary but not sufficient for safe deployment; sociotechnical integration, procurement governance, and replacement of required trial evidence are out of scope.

A prospective pilot is proposed with a primary endpoint (heterogeneity of evidence across gates) and secondary operational endpoints (override frequency, remediation time, failure concentration, concordance).
manuscript_anchors: From Theory to Empirical Testing: Pilot Design

claims.statement: Limitations include lack of prospective live evaluation, single-author audit reproducibility uncertainty, conservative coding that may overstate the gap, and provisional numeric defaults (e.g., override windows, escalation triggers) requiring empirical calibration.
